message.content:
FIX-C (judgement fixes, opus-scoped), reporting to Rob. Repo /Users/amanigga/Desktop/TAMAZIA-REBUILD/tamazia-audit-engine, branch p3-evidence @ HEAD. Read CONSTITUTION.md + caution.md C-200..C-208 + docs/p3-wave2-reports/ROB-INTEGRATION-LEDGER.md first. GOAL: every SUBSTANTIVE CodeRabbit finding on PR #4 implemented or reasoned-defer with a maintainer reply drafted. Fetch CodeRabbit's OWN findings (exclude the cs-code-health CodeScene annotations): curl -H "Authorization: Bearer GHP_TOKEN_REDACTED" "https://api.github.com/repos/Tamaziaa/tamazia-audit-engine/pulls/4/comments?per_page=100" paginated, filter user.login == "coderabbitai[bot]" AND body NOT starting with "[//]: # (cs-code-health)"; also GET .../pulls/4/reviews for its review bodies (outside-diff comments ride there).

RULES OF ENGAGEMENT: fail-closed bias (this codebase always prefers refusing over guessing); official-source verification via WebFetch before ANY change to legal/pack content (do not edit catalogue/packs without it); the artifact-type enum, VERDICTS underscore, safe-path/safe-fetch single doors are BINDING (ledger) - a finding that asks to violate one gets a reasoned-defer reply instead. OWNERSHIP: you own whatever files the findings touch EXCEPT complexity-only refactors (FIX-B) and code-scanning-alert sites (FIX-A) - where a finding overlaps their lanes, implement the SEMANTIC part and note the overlap. Match every verdict to the HEAD SHA (C-202: check comment is on current code, not stale). Tests for every fail-closed change.
BENCHMARKS: npm test green (exact counts), lint green, sweep GREEN, --strict green, e2e --breach-inline RESULT: OK, health-gate zero NEW violations. Do not git commit. British English, no em dashes, no new deps. REPORT to Rob: per-finding table (path:line -> severity -> action: fixed+test | deferred+drafted-reply | overlap-handoff), exit codes, honest gaps.

- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

1	# CONSTITUTION: tamazia-audit-engine
2	
3	These are the mechanically enforced rules of this repository. Every rule exists because the old estate broke it and shipped a false legal claim, a phantom mint, or a confident zero. A rule with no enforcing gate is theatre, so every rule below names the CI gate or tool that enforces it. If a gate cannot fire, the rule does not exist: fix the gate before writing code that depends on the rule.
4	
5	Enforcement-file status: this repo is at P0, so some named enforcing files are the committed names later phases must land, not files in the tree today. The Part III gate map marks every file **live** or **planned (Pn)**; a prose "Enforced by" reference to a file not yet in the tree means the planned file in that map.
6	
7	This repository is PUBLIC. No secret, token, key, password or connection string may ever appear in any file. This document is not legal advice and the engine's output is not legal advice; a standing not-legal-advice line ships on every rendered audit.
8	
9	Sources: docs/PRD.md §3, docs/discovery/digest-research-llm-agents.md, docs/discovery/digest-research-compliance.md, docs/discovery/digest-claude-ledger-forensic.md, docs/discovery/digest-findings-bible.md.
10	
11	---
12	
13	## Part I: The constitutional rules
14	
15	### Rule 1: One door per fact
16	Every client-facing fact (legal name, jurisdiction, sector, regulator, fine, law name, host, element checklist) has exactly ONE producer module. Consumers read the fact; they never re-derive it. The renderer introduces no facts.
17	
18	**Why:** the old estate had 10 producers of jurisdiction, 8 of host, 6 of sector, 5 of regulator; the stale door is the one the client sees. This class shipped a P0 three times: the ghost jurisdiction, "Sector regulator" printed on 51% of the catalogue, and the £17.5M fine fixed in the DB that never reached the client because three code files kept their own copy (digest-findings-bible §B1).
19	
20	**Enforced by:** `tools/one-door/check.js` (blocking gate, ported from cowork-os PR #342) run inside the sweep (`.github/workflows/sweep.yml` and `tools/sweep/run.js`, wired into `.github/workflows/ci.yml`) on every PR; `tools/fact-lineage/check.js` asserts a single producer per fact and that the renderer imports facts only through the payload contract (activates fully when `payload/schema/facts-lineage.json` lands in P4).
21	
22	### Rule 2: Catalogue-only law facts
23	The compiled catalogue artifact is the ONLY source of law names, citations, fines, penalty bands, regulators and enforcement intel. A literal law name, fine amount or regulator string in engine or renderer code fails CI.
24	
25	**Why:** hand-maintained copies drifted for months: `FW_NAME_CAT` was a frozen 285-code snapshot rendering "Uae Newcode"; `fineRate` regexes applied wrong ceilings to non-fining bodies; the self-learning loop invented statutes with fake legislation.gov.uk URLs (digest-claude-ledger-forensic b64, b65, b97).
26	
27	**Enforced by:** `catalogue/lint/no-literal-law-facts.js` (blocking, scans engine + renderer source for law-name/fine/regulator literals outside `catalogue/`) in `.github/workflows/catalogue-lint.yml`; the render truth-pack in `render-proof/truth-pack.spec.js` rejects any rendered fine, regulator or law title not present in the compiled catalogue.
28	
29	### Rule 3: No artifact, no breach
30	A breach finding must carry a deterministic artifact: a verbatim quote string-matched to the crawled corpus, a captured network event, a register row, or a failing DOM node. No artifact means the finding cannot exist, whatever a regex or a model said.
31	
32	**Why:** every breach Tamazia ever sent before v23.0 was an unreviewed regex match no model or artifact ever verified; a bare-word regex over raw HTML accused a real criminal-defence law firm of being hacked ("sex discrimination", `<slot>`) (digest-claude-ledger-forensic b84, e-adjudicator). Pentest doctrine: "a finding with no reproduction steps cannot be trusted" (digest-research-compliance §D).
33	
34	**Enforced by:** `breach/verifiers/` is the only path from proposer to adjudicator, and the payload schema in `payload/schema/` makes the artifact fields mandatory (missing artifact = schema-invalid payload, mint refused); `render-proof/truth-pack.spec.js` re-matches every rendered quote against the stored corpus word by word in `.github/workflows/render-truth.yml`.
35	
36	### Rule 4: Every gate fails closed and is calibrated against known-bad input
37	A gate that errors, times out or receives malformed input must BLOCK, not pass. Every analyser and gate must demonstrably fail on a seeded bad fixture on every run; a gate that has never fired is assumed broken.
38	
39	**Why:** mandatory audit gates ran AFTER the R2 payload was persisted; `llmPreflight()` failed open; the jurisdiction stage was marked `ran` unconditionally; three evidence layers threw ReferenceErrors on their first line for two whole versions while a fail-open catch shipped every audit as if the model had read the breaches (digest-findings-bible F-0001; digest-claude-ledger-forensic b106).
40	
41	**Enforced by:** `eval/calibration-known-bad/run.js` in `.github/workflows/ci.yml` feeds every gate a corpus of seeded bad inputs each run and fails CI if any gate passes one; `tools/swallow-gate/check.js` (AST-level, repo-wide, also in `.github/workflows/ci.yml`) blocks any catch block that swallows without recording a typed failure state.
42	
43	### Rule 5: Reachable or DORMANT
44	Every module is either provably reachable from the mint entry point or explicitly declared in `DORMANT.md` with a reason. Dead-but-green code is a lie waiting to ship.
45	
46	**Why:** 13 modules / 691 lines of correct, tested, cited legal logic were unreachable from the mint, including `citation-gate.js`, the gate built to make an unevidenced monetary claim impossible, which had never executed once; `statute-rag.js` was required for months and never called (digest-findings-bible §B6).
47	
48	**Enforced by:** madge + dependency-cruiser reachability walk in `.github/workflows/reachability.yml`, blocking: any module not reachable from `mint/` and not listed in `DORMANT.md` fails the PR; `tools/domain-gates/reachability.js` additionally asserts every ACTIVE catalogue rule routes to a scanner branch that can actually fire.
49	
50	### Rule 6: Below-confidence output is quarantined, never shipped
51	Anything the engine cannot assert with evidence at or above the confidence floor routes to quarantine (`needs-review` or `compliance_unassessed`), never into a shipped claim. Ambiguity defaults to withholding the accusation.
52	
53	**Why:** ambiguous LLM entailment verdicts ("Unclear, leaning no") matched neither branch and the finding stayed CONFIRMED with its fine; a foreign-language site got a fabricated 16-breach GDPR cascade because English regexes ran on a French corpus; unreadable bot-walled sites were asserted against (digest-claude-ledger-forensic b135, b1, b126).
54	
55	**Enforced by:** the adjudicator contract in `breach/adjudicator/` (verdict enum has no "maybe ships" branch: only violation / needs-review / pass, unparseable verdict = needs-review); `eval/calibration-known-bad/run.js` includes ambiguous-verdict and non-English fixtures that must land in quarantine; the payload schema rejects findings whose confidence fields are below floor but whose state is `violation`.
56	
57	### Rule 7: A mint is done only when row + HTTP 200 + truth-pack all pass
58	"Minted" means: the DB row exists, the live URL answers 200, and the Playwright truth-pass proves the rendered words match the payload. Anything less is not done, whatever the queue says.
59	
60	**Why:** the old queue reported "done" on 1,004 phantom audits; `ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING` adopted stale rows while the queue said done; a transport failure masqueraded as an idempotent conflict and a row that was never written was hunted for days (digest-claude-ledger-forensic b102, b103; PRD §1).
61	
62	**Enforced by:** `mint/post-write-assertions.js` runs inside every mint worker (row read-back + live 200 + truth-pack invocation before status flips to done); `tools/mint-reconciler/reconcile.js` in `.github/workflows/mint-reconcile.yml` (scheduled) diffs queue vs audit_pages vs leads and alarms on any done-without-page.
63	
64	### Rule 8: Budgets are caps, never floors
65	Every page, token, retry and time budget is an upper bound. No `Math.max` floors, no minimum waits, no "at least N seconds".
66	
67	**Why:** the SPA-render tail carried a 45-second FLOOR via `Math.max`, so a few shells always cost 45s or more and the ~45s-any-website capability was lost; the measured fix (E-236) restored a 43s crawl to 6.9s with identical accuracy (digest-claude-ledger-forensic §d).
68	
69	**Enforced by:** `tools/domain-gates/budget-caps.js` (AST scan for floor patterns on budget variables) in `.github/workflows/domain-gates.yml`; the Speed specialist's per-stage latency report in `eval/speed-budget.js` fails CI if any stage's configured budget behaves as a floor on the golden set.
70	
71	### Rule 9: Every external step has a hard deadline
72	Every LLM call, browser step, register lookup and fetch is wrapped in a hard `Promise.race` deadline. A slow dependency degrades the mint; it never hangs it.
73	
74	**Why:** a stuck Chromium held a mint hostage for 752 seconds because the 30s goto timeout did not bound browser launch + networkidle; exhausted free LLM tiers burned 30s × 3 retries × 3 gate attempts waiting for answers that were never coming (digest-claude-ledger-forensic b115, §d E-238).
75	
76	**Enforced by:** `evidence/` and `llm/router.js` expose only deadline-wrapped call primitives (no raw fetch/browser call exports); `tools/domain-gates/deadline-audit.js` in `.github/workflows/domain-gates.yml` fails CI on any external call site not routed through a deadline wrapper; timeout behaviour is exercised by hang fixtures in `eval/calibration-known-bad/`.
77	
78	### Rule 10: Three-state findings, and confident voice must be EARNED
79	Every finding is exactly one of **violation / needs-review / pass** (the axe-core doctrine: anything not assertable with certainty degrades to needs-review, and every false positive is a bug). Voice policy (the punchier hybrid, Aman-decided 2026-07-16): the confident "evidenced on your live site" voice is reserved for register-verified facts and adjudicated findings carrying verbatim quotes; needs-review items render in observation language ("detected X; this may implicate Act §Y, penalty band Z"); a standing not-legal-advice line appears on every audit. No finding is ever phrased as an adjudicated legal conclusion.
80	
81	**Why:** binary verdicts forced the verifier to call the real Equality Act fabricated because it could not say "unverifiable"; low-confidence attachments rendered as hard breaches; invented enforcement filler printed when no curated intel existed (digest-claude-ledger-forensic b100, b59, b96; digest-research-compliance §A axe-core, lesson 15).
82	
83	**Enforced by:** the payload schema (`payload/schema/`) types `finding.state` as the closed three-value enum and ties voice tier to state plus evidence fields (confident-voice strings on a needs-review finding = schema-invalid); `render-proof/truth-pack.spec.js` asserts the not-legal-advice line is present and that no needs-review finding renders breach-voice phrasing; `eval/golden/run.js` locks voice regressions.
84	
85	### Rule 11: The LLM is extraction and selection only; it never authors a fact
86	The LLM may select among retrieved candidate spans, extract from supplied material, and adjudicate proposed findings as a FILTER (it can remove or downgrade, never invent). It never writes a law name, fine, citation, jurisdiction or breach into existence. All LLM output is structured (schema-enforced), logged in full, and every prompt/model change is gated on a golden set built at least 60% from real production failures. Temperature 0 is not determinism: log inputs, outputs and model build for every call.
87	
88	**Why:** commercial RAG-backed legal tools still hallucinate in 1 of 6 or more queries (Stanford RegLab); our own self-learning loop invented "Cookies... Regulations 2020" with three different fabricated URLs and resurrected the repealed Disability Discrimination Act 1995 (digest-research-llm-agents Part A; digest-claude-ledger-forensic b97).
89	
90	**Enforced by:** `llm/gate.js` (ported, proven live: deterministic rubrics, never model self-confidence, three strikes to deterministic fallback) wraps every call; the adjudicator's filter-only contract is tested against a hallucinated-id fixture in `llm/evals/run.js` (`.github/workflows/llm-eval.yml`, blocking on precision and abstain-rate); full call logging asserted by `eval/calibration-known-bad/` replay fixtures.
91	
92	### Rule 12: The five structural-impossibility LLM gates (fail-closed AND-chain)
93	Every LLM-touched claim must pass ALL five gates. They compose: gates 1 and 2 are deterministic set/string operations, so fabricated citations and fake quotes have escape probability zero, not merely small. Everything rejected routes to abstention; the failure mode is "refuses to answer", never "confidently wrong".
94	
95	**Gate 1: Retrieval-gated emission.** A claim may cite only a `source_id` present in the injected retrieval set; a citation to a non-retrieved authority is unrepresentable in the output schema.
96	*Why:* 13-21% of legal citations across five commercial systems were hallucinated (arXiv 2606.00898). *Enforced by:* structured-output schemas in `llm/prompts/` whose source_id fields are constrained to the injected candidate list, validated post-hoc by `llm/gate.js` (out-of-set id = score 0, reject).
97	
98	**Gate 2: Verbatim-quote exact re-match.** Every quoted span must survive a normalised exact-substring match against the cited document before the claim may exist.
99	*Why:* models paraphrase inside quotation marks and fabricate pincites (Stanford HAI); our corpus regex ran on raw HTML while the quote came from stripped text, so hit and quote disagreed (digest-claude-ledger-forensic b32). *Enforced by:* `breach/verifiers/quote-match.js` (deterministic, blocking, one detection surface per rule); re-verified at render by `render-proof/truth-pack.spec.js`.
100	
101	**Gate 3: NLI entailment per claim.** Each atomic claim is the hypothesis, its cited span the premise; anything not labelled entailment is rejected.
102	*Why:* fluent-but-neutral sentences are the classic grounded hallucination (Nature 2024 semantic entropy; Google NLI research). *Enforced by:* the entailment step in `breach/adjudicator/` with its verdict rubric scored in `llm/gate.js`; calibration fixtures with known neutral/contradiction pairs in `llm/evals/` must be rejected every run.
103	
104	**Gate 4: Abstain-by-default confidence floor.** The default output is insufficient-evidence; a claim releases only above a calibrated floor, and abstention is always the cheaper path.
105	*Why:* forcing an answer on every query "causes confident mistakes and hallucinations" (SelectLLM); ambiguous verdicts defaulting to CONFIRMED kept fines on findings (digest-claude-ledger-forensic b135). *Enforced by:* the floor constant lives in `breach/adjudicator/` and is regression-locked by `llm/evals/run.js` abstain-rate gates; lowering it without updating the eval set fails CI.
106	
107	**Gate 5: Diverse jury with veto-to-reject.** P0/P1 findings require a quorum of genuinely independent model families; any single veto rejects; the jury may never veto curated catalogue facts (SECTOR_CORE / SECTOR_AGNOSTIC immunity).
108	*Why:* weak judges have high true-positive but very low true-negative rates, so a rejection is more trustworthy than an approval; our "dual-model" cross-check once hit the same provider twice and was not independent; the LLM was once allowed to veto the SRA off a solicitors' firm (digest-research-llm-agents Pattern 5; digest-claude-ledger-forensic b73, b91). *Enforced by:* `llm/router.js` quorum configuration asserts distinct provider families; immunity sets tested in `llm/evals/run.js`; forked verifier legs share one guarded code path (the b92 lesson) checked by `tools/one-door/check.js`.
109	
110	### Rule 13: Serves is not bound: the Tier A/B/C jurisdiction matrix
111	A jurisdiction attaches only on evidence, graded by tier: **Tier A** (register row, regulatory authorisation, registered office) scores 5; **Tier B** (office postcode, local phone, ccTLD/hreflang, local currency) scores 3; **Tier C** (prose mentions, "we serve clients in...") scores 1. Attachment requires one Tier A or two Tier B signals. Tier C NEVER attaches law; at most it feeds an advisory tier. Serving a market is not being bound by its law; law applicability is then a pure set-membership query over the applicability matrix (jurisdiction × sector × activity tags), and the verdict is graded with an evidence chain, KYB-style, never a boolean.
112	
113	**Why:** `/incorporated in/` anchored to no country judged 6 of 7 real UK/EU/UAE law-firm footers "established in the United States", and Mills & Reeve (UK) was served US ABA rules and ADA law, 4 of 8 findings jurisdictionally void; template hreflang pulled EU law onto a US clinic; a jurisdiction statement told a UAE firm the CCPA applied (digest-findings-bible DG-05; digest-claude-ledger-forensic b55, b128; digest-research-compliance §C).
114	
115	**Enforced by:** `facts/jurisdiction.js` is the single door for jurisdiction (Rule 1 gate applies); `tools/domain-gates/nexus-anchoring.js` in `.github/workflows/domain-gates.yml` replays the known-bad footer corpus (the Mills & Reeve class) every run; `eval/reference-set/run.js` fails on any jurisdiction contradiction against the hand-verified firms.
116	
117	### Rule 14: Provenance-mandatory catalogue rows
118	No catalogue row (law, rule, penalty, enforcement case) exists without provenance: source, official URL verified by FETCHING it and confirming the page IS that law (a 200 with matching content, never a 202 or an unfetched string), and last_synced. Discovered candidates are three-state (verified / rejected / unverifiable); only verified rows are promoted, and promotion of legal judgements is human-gated.
119	
120	**Why:** the official-URL "gate" checked only that a URL string was present; legislation.gov.uk answers 202-with-empty-body to ANY URL shape, so the status-code gate passed every fabrication; a full discovery run over 151 candidates yielded zero promotable laws (digest-claude-ledger-forensic b97, b98, b99).
121	
122	**Enforced by:** `catalogue/lint/provenance.js` and `catalogue/lint/citation-completeness.js` (blocking) in `.github/workflows/catalogue-lint.yml`: every row must carry resolvable provenance fields and every ACTIVE rule a citation; the catalogue compiler in `catalogue/` refuses to emit an artifact containing an unverified row; enforcement-intel population is human-gated per `catalogue/lint/enforcement-crossval.js`.
123	
124	---
125	
126	## Part II: Supplementary mechanical rules (earned by specific failures)
127	
128	### Rule 15: No scan cache; the engine version is load-bearing
129	A legal-evidence engine keeps no scan cache (a day-old scan dated today is not evidence). Any change to scan logic bumps ENGINE_VERSION, the version rides the idempotency key, and the DB-level trigger keyed on `engine_flags.required_engine_version` is the lock every minter must pass.
130	
131	**Why:** `scanner_cache` replayed pre-fix scans so "it's fixed now" was false four rounds running; a rogue pre-gate Hetzner cron minted on v19 for days; a partial catalogue (290 frameworks, 0 rules) was cached process-wide and connect attached nothing (digest-claude-ledger-forensic §c, b44, b107).
132	
133	**Enforced by:** `.github/workflows/engine-version-guard.yml` fails any PR touching scan logic without a version bump; the DB gate is ported as part of `mint/`; `tools/mint-reconciler/reconcile.js` alarms on any row minted under a stale version.
134	
135	### Rule 16: No secrets, ever (public repo)
136	Nothing matching a credential shape (ghp_*, npg_*, sk-*, cfut_*, xoxb-*, postgres://, or any key/token/password) may appear in any file, fixture, log capture or golden payload. Golden fixtures are structural-only and pseudonymised; raw payloads with prospect PII stay gitignored.
137	
138	**Why:** a leaked webhook secret persisted verbatim in a harvested review snapshot on the old estate (digest-findings-bible GR-0002/CR-0010); golden fixtures nearly leaked prospect PII into a public repo (digest-claude-ledger-forensic b122).
139	
140	**Enforced by:** `.github/workflows/secret-scan.yml` (gitleaks, blocking, full history on push) plus GitHub push protection; `eval/golden/` fixture linter rejects email addresses and person names outside the pseudonym dictionary.
141	
142	### Rule 17: Done means verified against ground truth
143	No agent, script or session reports a change done until verified at the end against ground truth: the git diff, the DB row, the live minted payload, the deployed asset. In-loop assumptions ("already present, skip") are not verification.
144	
145	**Why:** a 17-node sector insert was reported done three times while silently skipping; PR #114 on the website was an empty merge whose "deploy success" shipped nothing; a fix merged to one leg while the quorum leg kept the bug (digest-claude-ledger-forensic b117, b118, §c).
146	
147	**Enforced by:** Rule 7's mint reconciler for engine output; `render-proof/truth-pack.spec.js` for rendered output; CI status on the actual merge commit is the only accepted "done" signal per AGENTS.md Fleet Rule 1.
148	
149	---
150	
151	## Part III: The gate map (rule → enforcing file)
152	
153	**Legend:** files LIVE today: `tools/one-door/check.js`, `tools/swallow-gate/check.js`, `tools/fact-lineage/check.js`, `tools/sweep/*`, `eval/calibration-known-bad/run.js`, `eval/golden/run.js`, `eval/reference-set/verify.js`, `payload/contract/index.js`, `payload/schema/payload.schema.json`, and workflows `ci.yml`, `sweep.yml`, `codeql.yml`, `semgrep.yml`. Every other file in this table is **PLANNED (P1+)**: it is the committed name the owning phase must land, and this table is the contract for it. A planned gate is not an excuse — the rule binds from day one; until its dedicated file lands, the closest live gate (sweep/ci) covers what it can and the rest is enforced in review.
154	
155	| Rule | Enforcing gate | File |
156	|---|---|---|
157	| 1 One door per fact | one-door + fact-lineage | `tools/one-door/check.js` (live, runs in ci/sweep), `tools/fact-lineage/check.js` (live, arms fully in P4) |
158	| 2 Catalogue-only law facts | catalogue lint + truth-pack | `catalogue/lint/no-literal-law-facts.js` (planned P2), `render-proof/truth-pack.spec.js` (planned P4); today `tools/one-door/check.js` (live) blocks fine/regulator/law-title literals outside `catalogue/` |
159	| 3 No artifact no breach | payload schema + verifiers | `payload/schema/payload.schema.json` (live), `breach/verifiers/` (planned P3), `.github/workflows/render-truth.yml` (planned P4) |
160	| 4 Fail-closed calibrated gates | known-bad corpus + silent-swallow AST | `eval/calibration-known-bad/run.js` (live, in ci.yml), `tools/swallow-gate/check.js` (live, in ci.yml + sweep) |
161	| 5 Reachable-or-DORMANT | reachability walk | `DORMANT.md` (lands with the first module), reachability walk live in `tools/sweep/collect-local.js` (arms when `mint/worker.js` or `mint/index.js` exists); madge + dependency-cruiser live in ci.yml |
162	| 6 Quarantine below confidence | adjudicator contract + fixtures | `breach/adjudicator/` (planned P3), `eval/calibration-known-bad/run.js` (live) |
163	| 7 Mint = row+200+truth-pack | post-write assertions + reconciler | `mint/post-write-assertions.js`, `tools/mint-reconciler/reconcile.js`, `.github/workflows/mint-reconcile.yml` (all planned P4) |
164	| 8 Budgets are caps | budget-caps gate + speed report | `tools/domain-gates/budget-caps.js`, `eval/speed-budget.js`, `.github/workflows/domain-gates.yml` (all planned P3) |
165	| 9 Hard external deadlines | deadline-audit gate | `tools/domain-gates/deadline-audit.js` (planned P3), hang fixtures in `eval/calibration-known-bad/` |
166	| 10 Three-state + earned voice | payload schema + truth-pack + golden | `payload/schema/payload.schema.json` (live), `render-proof/truth-pack.spec.js` (planned P4), `eval/golden/run.js` (live) |
167	| 11 LLM extraction/selection only | gate rubric + LLM eval harness | `llm/gate.js`, `llm/evals/run.js`, `.github/workflows/llm-eval.yml` (all planned P3) |
168	| 12 The 5 structural gates | schema constraint, quote-match, entailment, floor, quorum | `llm/prompts/` schemas, `breach/verifiers/quote-match.js`, `breach/adjudicator/`, `llm/router.js`, `llm/evals/run.js` (all planned P3) |
169	| 13 Tier A/B/C jurisdiction | nexus-anchoring + reference set | `facts/jurisdiction.js` (planned P2), `tools/domain-gates/nexus-anchoring.js` (planned P3), `eval/reference-set/verify.js` (live) |
170	| 14 Provenance-mandatory catalogue | provenance + citation lints | `catalogue/lint/provenance.js`, `catalogue/lint/citation-completeness.js` (all planned P2) |
171	| 15 No cache + version gate | engine-version guard + DB trigger | `.github/workflows/engine-version-guard.yml` (planned P4), `mint/` DB gate (planned P4) |
172	| 16 No secrets | secret scan + fixture linter | Semgrep p/secrets pack (live, `.github/workflows/semgrep.yml`); `.github/workflows/secret-scan.yml` gitleaks lane + `eval/golden/` fixture linter (planned P1) |
173	| 17 Done = ground truth | reconciler + truth-pack + CI status | `tools/mint-reconciler/reconcile.js`, `render-proof/truth-pack.spec.js` (planned P4); CI status on the merge commit (live) |
174	
175	Also always-on (the 12-tool sweep, per PRD §7): CodeQL (`.github/workflows/codeql.yml`, live), Semgrep (`.github/workflows/semgrep.yml`, live), CodeRabbit and CodeScene (GitHub Apps, least-privilege, cannot write code; founder enables per docs/FOUNDER-ACTIONS.md), madge + dependency-cruiser (live in `.github/workflows/ci.yml`), jscpd and ESLint (live in `.github/workflows/ci.yml`), the sweep orchestrator (`.github/workflows/sweep.yml`, live nightly), the domain gates (`.github/workflows/domain-gates.yml`, planned P3), and the Playwright render lane (`.github/workflows/render-truth.yml`, planned P4). SARIF fan-in, fingerprint dedupe, DSU clustering and the numbered ledger live in `tools/sweep/`. ACT (two or more independent tools agree) blocks all progress until fixed; a single-tool finding is a lead, never auto-fixed.

content.content:
443:**C-200** Findings were phrased as adjudicated legal conclusions -> findings ship as evidence-quoted factual observations plus risk indicators ("detected X; this may implicate Act §Y, penalty band Z") with a standing not-legal-advice line; conclusory phrasing fails the render truth-pack. If you would not assert it in a tribunal, do not render it.
445:**C-201** (2026-07-16, P0) The very first CI run failed on a tree divergence local never saw: dependency-cruiser enumerated scaffold directories that git does not track when empty, so CI checked out a different tree than the one verified locally -> every gate must run against the tree git actually ships (empty intended directories carry .gitkeep; local verification is done from a fresh `git clone` of HEAD, not the working directory, before declaring a phase green).
447:**C-202** (2026-07-17, P2) An unquoted ": " inside a ci.yml step name broke the workflow YAML; GitHub does not fail an unparsable workflow - its checks simply VANISH from the check list, and "all present checks green" was read as a full pass while the entire local fleet never ran -> (1) tools/sweep/collect-workflows.js parses every workflow file as a P0 lane; (2) a green run is only green when the check-run LIST matches the expected manifest, never merely when the present checks pass; (3) the main ruleset requires the named contexts, so a vanished check blocks merge structurally.
449:**C-203** (2026-07-17, P2) THRESHOLD_RX matched the bare word "employee" inside "endorsement by an employee", flagging a size threshold on a law about endorsement authorship (US_FTC_REVIEWS_ENDORSEMENTS) -> a vocabulary keyword is only a signal WITH its semantic context (count/number/threshold qualifier or leading numeral); every such tightening lands with calibration fixtures in BOTH directions (true positive still fires, known false positive stays quiet).
451:**C-204** (2026-07-17, P2) Session/quota cuts killed agent fleets mid-write three times; one fleet's work was nearly redone from scratch before checking the tree -> salvage doctrine: after any cut, inventory the working tree + partial outputs FIRST, relaunch with salvage-tolerant prompts that read-and-complete rather than rewrite; never restart work that survived. Quota-heavy phases route workers to sonnet/opus, never fable.
453:**C-205** (2026-07-17, P2) QA sidecar hash-stamps were worded as release "approvals" although the hash only proves the pack is unchanged since legal review; an agent attestation is not an authority -> sidecars are INTEGRITY ATTESTATIONS; release = CI green + founder merge, and no agent-authored artefact may claim approval semantics (CI is the only arbiter of done, an agent's opinion is never a merge signal).
455:**C-206** (2026-07-17, P2) A CodeScene multi-entry web form silently dropped batched entries (UI race) and a prior agent's staged entries were lost because the final submit never happened -> UI configuration work must re-read the persisted state after EVERY submit and verify each entry individually; staged-but-unsubmitted state counts as not done.
457:**C-207** (2026-07-17, P2) New tooling landed inside an open PR reset its review cycle twice (each new gate/tool the reviewers had never seen drew a fresh round of findings on the same PR, so it never converged) -> tooling/enabler code ships through its OWN gate-loop PR; an open feature PR only ever SHRINKS (fixes to what is already there), never grows new tooling surface. A feature PR that keeps adding gates re-opens its own review indefinitely.
466:4. **The seed set (C-001 to C-200) is dated 2026-07-16**, mined from: digest-claude-ledger-forensic.md (135 raw pointers), digest-findings-bible.md, digest-plan-docs.md, digest-architecture-canon.md, digest-live-estate.md, digest-website-render.md, digest-llm-callsites.md, AUDIT-ENGINE-FAILURE-CATALOGUE.md (the 9-audit A1-G3 taxonomy), AUDIT-ENGINE-2-MONTH-FORENSIC-2026-07-14.md (the 5 earned rules and 12 recommendations), and the old repo's DORMANT.md.

python3 << 'EOF'
import re, json
from collections import Counter
SP="/private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-amanigga-Desktop-TAMAZIA-REBUILD-Tamazia-Remix/9371914b-1c34-41af-ba44-11ed044a2ff3/scratchpad"

def parse_findings(text, source):
    findings=[]
    file_re = re.compile(r'<summary>([^<\n]+?\.[A-Za-z0-9]+)\s*\((\d+)\)</summary>')
    files=[(m.start(), m.group(1)) for m in file_re.finditer(text)]
    def filefor(pos):
        f=None
        for s,p in files:
            if s<pos: f=p
            else: break
        return f
    # entry: optional-backtick LINE optional-backtick colon _cat_ | _sev_ | _effort_
    entry_re = re.compile(r'`?(\d+(?:-\d+)?)`?:\s*_([^_\n]+)_\s*\|\s*_([^_\n]+)_\s*\|\s*_([^_\n]+)_', re.S)
    ms=list(entry_re.finditer(text))
    for i,m in enumerate(ms):
        seg = text[m.end(): ms[i+1].start() if i+1<len(ms) else len(text)]
        tm = re.search(r'\*\*(.+?)\*\*', seg, re.S)
        findings.append({
            "source":source,
            "path":filefor(m.start()),
            "line":m.group(1),
            "severity":m.group(3).strip(),
            "title":(tm.group(1).strip() if tm else "(no title)")[:140],
        })
    return findings

I'm seeing the full breakdown now: 129 unique inline findings that posted successfully, 39 that failed to post inline (which are distinct from the successful ones), and 5 findings outside the diff including some transcript files leaking local paths.

The path leaks showing usernames are a data-hygiene concern but not credentials—and since the repo is public anyway and these are just evidence artifacts, I could reasonably defer that. Now I need to merge the 39 failed-to-post findings with the 129 inline ones, deduplicating by path and title, then build a comprehensive master triage list with full body text for each unique finding so I can actually work through them.

With potentially ~170 findings after dedup, I need to be strategic about scope: my lane is judgement fixes (opus-scoped), so I own whatever files the findings touch except for complexity-only refactors (FIX-B) and code-scanning-alert sites (FIX-A). python3 << 'EOF'
import re, json
from collections import defaultdict, Counter
SP="/private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-amanigga-Desktop-TAMAZIA-REBUILD-Tamazia-Remix/9371914b-1c34-41af-ba44-11ed044a2ff3/scratchpad"

def title(body):
    m = re.search(r'\*\*(.+?)\*\*', body)
    return m.group(1).strip() if m else "(no title)"
def sevof(body):
    first = body.split("\n",1)[0]
    m = re.findall(r'_([^_]+)_', first)
    return m[1] if len(m)>=2 else "?"
def catof(body):
    first = body.split("\n",1)[0]
    m = re.findall(r'_([^_]+)_', first)
    return m[0] if len(m)>=1 else "?"

# strip the noisy Tools/Analysis details blocks to shorten body for digest
def clean(body):
    # remove <details>...</details> blocks (tools, analysis chains)
    b = re.sub(r'<details>\s*<summary>🧰 Tools</summary>.*?</details>\s*</details>', '', body, flags=re.S)
    b = re.sub(r'<details>\s*<summary>🧩 Analysis chain</summary>.*?</details>', '', b, flags=re.S)
    b = re.sub(r'<details>\s*<summary>🪛[^<]*</summary>.*?</details>', '', b, flags=re.S)
    b = re.sub(r'<details>\s*<summary>📓[^<]*</summary>.*?</details>', '', b, flags=re.S)
    b = re.sub(r'\n{3,}', '\n\n', b)
    return b.strip()

cr = json.load(open(f"{SP}/cr_uniq.json"))
merged=[]
for c in cr:
    merged.append({
        "path":c["path"], "line":c.get("line"), "start_line":c.get("start_line"),
        "severity":sevof(c["body"]), "category":catof(c["body"]),
        "title":title(c["body"]), "body":clean(c["body"]), "src":"inline",
        "html_url":c.get("html_url")
    })

# failed-to-post full bodies
t=open(f"{SP}/review_4728255381.md").read()
idx=t.find("Comments failed to post")
tt=t[idx:]
file_re = re.compile(r'<summary>([^<\n]+?\.[A-Za-z0-9]+)\s*\((\d+)\)</summary>')
files=[(m.start(), m.group(1)) for m in file_re.finditer(tt)]
def filefor(pos):
    f=None
    for s,p in files:
        if s<pos: f=p
        else: break
    return f
entry_re = re.compile(r'`?(\d+(?:-\d+)?)`?:\s*_([^_\n]+)_\s*\|\s*_([^_\n]+)_\s*\|\s*_([^_\n]+)_', re.S)
ms=list(entry_re.finditer(tt))
for i,m in enumerate(ms):
    seg = tt[m.end(): ms[i+1].start() if i+1<len(ms) else len(tt)]
    tm = re.search(r'\*\*(.+?)\*\*', seg, re.S)
    merged.append({
        "path":filefor(m.start()), "line":m.group(1), "start_line":None,
        "severity":m.group(3).strip(), "category":m.group(2).strip(),
        "title":(tm.group(1).strip() if tm else "(no title)"),
        "body":clean(seg), "src":"failed-to-post", "html_url":None
    })

# dedupe merged by (path, title)
seen={}
for f in merged:
    k=(f["path"], f["title"])
    if k not in seen or len(f["body"])>len(seen[k]["body"]):
        seen[k]=f
merged=list(seen.values())
print(f"Total merged unique findings: {len(merged)}")
print("By severity:", dict(Counter(f["severity"] for f in merged)))
print("By category:", dict(Counter(f["category"] for f in merged)))

# group by file, write digest
sevorder={"🔴 Critical":0,"🟠 Major":1,"🟡 Minor":2,"🔵 Trivial":3}
byfile=defaultdict(list)
for f in merged: byfile[f["path"]].append(f)
out=[]
for path in sorted(byfile):
    fs=sorted(byfile[path], key=lambda x:sevorder.get(x["severity"],9))
    out.append(f"\n\n{'='*90}\n### FILE: {path}  ({len(fs)} findings)\n{'='*90}")
    for i,f in enumerate(fs):
        out.append(f"\n--- [{path} :: {f['severity']} :: {f['category']}] line {f['line']} (src={f['src']}) ---")
        out.append(f"TITLE: {f['title']}")
        out.append(f["body"][:2200])
open(f"{SP}/DIGEST.md","w").write("\n".join(out))
json.dump(merged, open(f"{SP}/cr_master.json","w"), indent=2)
print("wrote DIGEST.md and cr_master.json")
# file counts
print("\nFindings per file (sorted):")
for path in sorted(byfile, key=lambda p:-len(byfile[p])):
    sevs=Counter(x["severity"] for x in byfile[path])
    print(f"  {len(byfile[path]):2d}  {path}   {dict(sevs)}")
EOF

I'm looking at a large digest with 167 findings that would be too token-heavy to process all at once in a single pass. My strategy is to read through the digest in chunks to build a triage table, then evaluate each finding as either something to fix, defer, or mark as overlapping with others, and finally group the actual fixes by file.

Many of these findings are duplicates described slightly differently—like the same issue appearing as both an inline finding and a failed-to-post finding with different wording—so the real unique count is probably closer to 100-110 distinct issues rather than the full 167. I'm considering whether to parallelize this work across subagents to handle clusters of files, though I need to be careful that they make sound judgement calls on fail-closed code changes rather than cutting corners.

Actually, I realize I'm the Opus agent that Rob launched specifically for these judgement fixes, so I should own the core decisions myself while using subagents only for verification and parallel implementation of well-specified fixes. Given the fail-closed bias and binding contracts involved, I'll work through this methodically but efficiently—reading the full digest, building a complete triage, then batch-implementing the fixes. Since the structured JSON is available, I should generate a compact digest that strips away all the boilerplate and keeps just the essential title and description for each finding, which will be far more scannable than the full 8289-line output.

import re, json
from collections import defaultdict
SP="/private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-amanigga-Desktop-TAMAZIA-REBUILD-Tamazia-Remix/9371914b-1c34-41af-ba44-11ed044a2ff3/scratchpad"
merged=json.load(open(f"{SP}/cr_master.json"))

def core(body):
    # cut everything from the first "<details>" AI prompt / suggestion onwards, and strip html comments
    b = body
    for marker in ["<details>\n<summary>🤖", "<details>\n<summary>📍", "<details>\n<summary>📝",
                   "<!-- suggestion_start", "<!-- consolidated_sites", "<!-- fingerprint",
                   "<details>\n<summary>Proposed fix", "<details>\n<summary>🧰"]:
        i=b.find(marker)
        if i>=0: b=b[:i]
    # keep proposed-fix diff if short
    b=re.sub(r'<!--.*?-->','',b,flags=re.S)
    b=re.sub(r'\n{3,}','\n',b).strip()
    return b

sevorder={"🔴 Critical":0,"🟠 Major":1,"🟡 Minor":2,"🔵 Trivial":3}
byfile=defaultdict(list)
for f in merged: byfile[f["path"]].append(f)
out=[]
n=0
for path in sorted(byfile):
    fs=sorted(byfile[path], key=lambda x:sevorder.get(x["severity"],9))
    out.append(f"\n### {path}")
    for f in fs:
        n+=1
        c=core(f["body"])
        # remove the leading severity line and bold-title repeat
        c=re.sub(r'^_[^\n]*_\n','',c)
        out.append(f"[{f['severity']}|{f['category']}|L{f['line']}] {c}")
open(f"{SP}/COMPACT.md","w").write("\n".join(out))
print(f"wrote COMPACT.md with {n} entries")
import os
print("size:", os.path.getsize(f"{SP}/COMPACT.md"))
EOF

=== health-gate check.js head ===
#!/usr/bin/env node
'use strict';
/**
 * HEALTH-GATE: the CodeScene-class complexity smells caught before a reviewer ever sees them.
 *
 * Builder agents ship functions that pass every test yet are the exact shape CodeScene later flags:
 * Complex Method (too long), Bumpy Road (too many independent branches), Deep Nested Complexity (too
 * many levels of if/for/while inside one another). This gate exists so those findings die in the
 * builder loop, before a reviewer or a CodeScene scan ever fires on them (caution.md C-163: "a gate
 * that cannot fire is theatre" - CodeScene refactoring-goal gates were enabled with no goals defined;
 * this gate has an explicit, calibrated goal from commit 1).
 *
 * Five caps, each a hard ceiling, never a target to creep toward:
 *   - function body        > 60 lines
 *   - nesting depth         > 4   (if/for/while/switch/try nested inside one another)
 *   - decision points       > 12  (if / for / while / do-while / switch-case / && / || / ?: )
 *   - formal parameters     > 5
 *   - file length           > 500 lines
 *
 * Engine: an AST walk via acorn. acorn is MANDATORY (it always ships as a transitive devDependency of
 * eslint). The old brace/indent fallback was removed in PR #3 because it under-reported template ${}
 * code, regex-literal braces and braceless nesting - all confident zeros. HEALTH_GATE_ENGINE=heuristic
 * now forces tools/health-gate/heuristic.js's EXPLICIT REFUSAL (it throws, check.js exits 2), so the
 * fail-closed fallback is exercised in CI (mirrors SWALLOW_GATE_ENGINE=regex, but this fallback refuses
 * rather than approximates - under-reporting is an unearned zero, Constitution Rule 4).
 *
 * Tests: node --test tools/health-gate/check.test.js
 *
 * Modes:
 *   node tools/health-gate/check.js                  scan the repo source tree, exit 1 on any violation
 *   node tools/health-gate/check.js --json <path>    also write findings JSON for the sweep normaliser
 *   node tools/health-gate/check.js --calibrate      scan eval/calibration-known-bad/fixtures/ and REQUIRE
 *                                                    that the seeded violations are found. Zero found = exit 1.
 */

content.thinking:
Looking at the baseline test results across the suite—npm tests are passing cleanly, linting checks out, the sweep tool is green, and the strict calibration passes. The e2e breach-inline test also comes back clean. There are some pre-existing violations flagged by the health gate in a few specific areas, but those aren't new issues.

The key constraint here is that I need to avoid introducing any new violations in the P3 files, especially around the adjudicate.js file which already has complexity concerns. CodeRabbit is flagging some functions as failing CodeScene checks, but those aren't actually showing up in the local health-gate baseline, so they're likely either stale findings or cloud-specific annotations. I'll treat those as lower-priority refactoring work rather than blocking issues.

Let me start organizing the actual semantic bugs I should tackle. The disproofMatches function in verdict.js is a real correctness issue—it's using a 40-character prefix match when it should require full disproof matching, which could allow fabrication bypasses. That's a genuine fail-closed bug worth fixing with regression tests.

For pattern-match.js, there are two concerns: the regexSourceOf function might have a ReDoS vulnerability if the catalogue regex isn't validated with safe-grammar rules (the ledger mentions this was supposedly fixed, but I need to verify the current state), and the malformed token handling at lines 93-101 treats empty or broken token sets as `all`, which is another fail-closed defect I should address.

Looking at propose.js now, there's a missing telemetry issue where isTruncated returns false without evidence, violating the coverage_proof contract that requires explicit truncation status. The type discriminator at line 310 can be overridden by the event object itself, which breaks the artifact-type canonicality requirement. And lines 388-389 discard browser and register observations when the corpus is empty or non-English, conflating the absence-of-data case with actual observations.

There's also a fail-open pattern where only specs are destructured while rejected data gets discarded, and several quote artifact and register substring inference issues tied to the one-door rule that need careful refactoring judgment.

In quote-match.js, when candidates isn't an array it defaults to empty, which passes validation incorrectly—that's a clear fail-closed fix. The dual quote-artifact contract at line 170 relates back to the propose.js producer issue and needs the same one-door judgment call.

Looking at register-row.js, the verifier throws on undefined artifacts instead of returning the proper missing fields error, which is defensively fail-closed but I need to verify if undefined artifacts actually occur in practice. And in network-event.js, the identity check only compares kind, host, name, and timestamp while ignoring the essential payload fields—that's a real integrity gap, though the ledger decision about consent artifacts reusing the observed-entry shape complicates whether this needs fixing.

For coverage-proof.js, I need to verify that the current implementation actually re-validates tier1_fetched, truncated, and pages_checked against the bundle as ledger decision 2 requires. And evidence-kind.js has a critical issue where absence_evidence.state can bypass the artifact requirement entirely by classifying as observation or register without a typed artifact—this violates the core rule that no artifact means no breach, though it intersects with the artifact-type enum mapping from ledger decision 1.

The legacy bypass in evidence-kind.js is the key question: is this a deliberate migration shim or a security hole? The tests explicitly assert this bypass behavior, so if I close it, the tests need updating. Then in adjudicate.js, there's a real integration bug where evidenceText and briefOf only read evidence_quote and absence_evidence but never artifact.text, meaning canonical quotes get presented as text-free absences and disproofs never match what propose.js generates.

In observe.js, timeouts and network errors are marked healthy:false and emit findings without status or error artifacts—they should abstain with healthy:null instead. That's a strong fail-closed fix. There's also an SSRF issue where control.url and nav URL reach I/O without parseSafeFetchTarget validation, which ties into both code scanning and the semantic safe-fetch-door rule about single-point parsing. The TRACKER_HOSTS export is a mutable Set that Object.freeze can't protect — I need to either export just an isTrackerHost() function or provide an immutable snapshot instead. There's also the question of whether those security cookie prefixes actually qualify as essential under the rules, and I'm leaning toward abstaining rather than classifying them that way. The hard-coded statutory citation is just catalogue material that shouldn't be there.

The overlap with catalogue-lint is tricky — CodeRabbit flagged the citation but the sweep came back clean, so moving it might be a heavier lift than it's worth right now. More pressing is the logic issue at L150 where cookie name overrides are letting PHPSESSID on doubleclick.net get marked as essential when it shouldn't be. And there's a coverage contract problem where obligations are marked covered without actually observing the page that would prove it.

Looking at the crawler coverage file, there's a sector slug hard-coding issue that violates the rule about pulling tags only from the vocabulary — that's a clear fix. Then in crawl.js, there's an evidence integrity bug where footer text gets extracted from the home HTML even when the page is actually a login screen or error page, which corrupts the data. There's also a budget handling issue where explicit zero values are being overridden with larger defaults.

The sitemap fetcher in crawl.js bypasses the deadline wrapper entirely, turning rejections into empty arrays instead of properly failing closed. In discover.js, the tier classification is checking the full URL including query parameters rather than just the path, so a domain like `privacy-services.example` would incorrectly get marked as Tier 1. There's also an SSRF vulnerability where sitemap destinations reach the fetch before the same-registrable-site check can filter them.

For the extract.js issue, unclosed script and style tags leave their contents in the evidence corpus, which means invisible text gets included in findings. The pool.js deadline boundary uses `>` when it should be `>=` to properly enforce the budget cap. I'm also looking at documents.js but the thought cuts off there.

Now examining the registers directory — HTTP failures with 404 status codes are incorrectly treated as successful fetches, which causes unparsed content to be treated as if the document was unreadable. Document budgets set to zero should act as hard caps. The regulator files are hardcoding sector slugs instead of pulling from the vocabulary, and regulator wording is scattered as literals rather than centralized in a catalogue.

The ico.js file has a date handling issue where malformed expiry dates fall back to the registered status, and date-only values are treated as expired from midnight. The registers.js fallback accepts raw sector values and hardcodes jurisdiction tags instead of using the vocabulary. The sra.js response handler creates matched rows with null sra_number when only a name is provided. The name-matching regex is too aggressive — it strips legal suffixes anywhere in the string rather than just at the end, which could incorrectly match things like "Limited Edition Design."

There's also a host parsing issue in the name-match fallback that bypasses the safe-fetch validation, a missing enforcement of the deadline cap in lib/deadline.js, and an unref() call that allows Node to exit before the timeout fires. In facts/identity.js, the entity form validation accepts ordinary words outside the suffix list, and the page-text corroboration logic needs examination.

Moving through the LLM modules, the entailment deadline isn't clamped to the budget cap, there's a missing guard for empty hypotheses, and the verdict response parsing has complexity that overlaps with other fixes. In the gate module, the collector silently passes unexamined claims when quotes lack a singular source node or exceed depth 12, which is a fail-closed issue.

The depth walker at line 255 skips subtrees beyond depth 13, allowing out-of-set citations to pass undetected—I need to record when max depth is exceeded. In the router, missing provider families are incorrectly counted as independent jurors, violating the distinct provider families gate, and the deadline resets per provider instead of aggregating across them, which breaks the hard deadline rule. The quorum decomposition logic also needs work but I'm deferring that for now.

Looking at the gates themselves, the budget regex pattern misses millisecond-suffixed variables like `closeMs` and `_MS`, so `DEFAULT_CLOSE_MS` incorrectly passes validation—this is a false-negative in the gate itself and needs both a fix and calibration tests. There's also a binding loss in nested budget expressions, and the deadline audit logic matches by property name rather than actual binding, which causes issues when Promise.race receives a single promise.

The host-parse exemption is too broad, covering the entire tools directory instead of just the safe-fetch entry point, and it only catches hardcoded variable names while computed member access like `url['includes']` slips through. Most of these are FIX candidates, though some like the AST decomposition work are deferred as heavier lifts.

Looking at the check.js gate itself, there are several coverage gaps: it doesn't track Set or Map constructors so `.add()` calls escape detection, the IIFE exemption is too permissive for nested cases, block scope shadowing isn't modeled, and the self-reference exemption catches too much. These are all gate-hardening opportunities to catch more defect patterns.

Now examining safe-fetch.js, there's a real SSRF vulnerability where hex-encoded IPv4-mapped IPv6 addresses like `::ffff:7f00:1` bypass the private-range check. This is a genuine security hole in the binding door itself, distinct from code-scanning alerts, so it's worth fixing with proper canonicalization and tests.

For the registrable-domain list in lines 179 and 163-179, the hand-written suffix list is missing github.io, appspot.com, and pages.dev — allowing tenant boundary crossing. Since adding new dependencies isn't allowed and CodeRabbit's suggestion to use the Public Suffix List is off the table, the safer approach is to fall back to exact-host matching when the suffix can't be determined safely. That's the fail-closed option and a good FIX candidate.

Moving into the eval/e2e harness findings, run-pipeline.js has an issue where recorded stage failures don't trigger non-zero exit codes. ] fail-open scenarios, catalogue records missing or malformed cause the system to proceed unsafely. Several pipeline issues compound this: fallback logic in adjudication bypasses the verifier contract, empty catalogues incorrectly mark breach lanes as complete with false confidence, and subprocess temp directory handling has security implications. There's also a documented scope limit in the redteam handlers where reports get flagged without actually running the observe browser lane.

Now I'm weighing whether CodeRabbit's requirement to run observe() or skip reporting is justified given that RT-G is explicitly marked as a documented scope limit in the fixture. The e2e output shows RT-F is already caught, so I need to verify if the stale xfail can be removed or if there's still an underlying identity contradiction to resolve.

Looking at the redteam.js implementation, there are several robustness issues: bundle failures reject the entire lane instead of handling per-fixture errors, caught reports fire after serialization failures, and unhandled throws in runGenericEntry crash the whole lane. I should wrap these in try-catch blocks and tighten error granularity. The test also shows that an empty catalogue produces zero proposals, which gets caught before the gates — that's working as intended.

Now I'm categorizing the remaining findings: some violate binding contracts and need to be deferred with reasoned explanations, while others like the evidence-kind.js legacy bypass don't actually conflict with the ledger decisions since those legacy literals are meant to become aliases under the canonical mapping. For the reference-set findings, I'm dealing with legal content that requires official-source verification before making changes. The reference set is the ground truth that the test harness is judged against, so altering it is risky. I should either verify these frameworks against official sources or reasonably defer the change with documented reasoning, especially since the reference set is deliberately curated and has a needs_verification flag for exactly this purpose.

Now I'm looking at PII issues in the fixture data—footerText fields containing staff emails and phone numbers that need redaction.  But changing the fixtures risks breaking the reference-set assertions that depend on them, so I'm leaning toward reasoned-defer unless the PII redaction is minimal and doesn't affect the test oracle. There's also some complexity in the LLM routing and entailment logic that overlaps with other fixes, so I'll defer those to the broader refactor.

I'm seeing a few more decomposition and split issues flagged by CodeScene cloud that our local health-gate doesn't catch — those are also complexity-only and should wait for the larger refactor effort. Then there's the GitHub Actions workflow pinning and security settings that Semgrep flagged, which looks like it belongs in the code-scanning lane, though I need to verify if that's actually in scope for the current fix batch. The pin-actions finding in the workflow file is actually a code-scanning-alert site since Semgrep flagged it, so that belongs to FIX-A's lane and I'll note the overlap. The functional issue about running the gate tests is mine to handle. For the SSRF findings across those files, I need to determine whether they're code-scanning-alert sites—meaning whether github-advanced-security actually flagged those specific lines.

I should pull the GHAS alerts to see exactly which files and line numbers they cover, so I can properly map the overlaps and avoid duplicating work. Looking at the scope realistically, with 167 findings total and over 100 real fixes needed, I can't implement everything comprehensively in one session without massive risk. I need to be strategic about prioritizing high-value fixes that I can actually complete and verify properly. I'm realizing the core tension: I need to triage and handle the high-judgment semantic fixes myself, but delegating the mechanical work to subagents risks violating the process rule that requires a quiet tree during verification stages. The safer approach is to sequence my work carefully—complete the implementation clusters first, then run verification after the build fleet settles, rather than having multiple agents editing simultaneously.

Given the scope of 167 findings across 78 files, I need to be realistic about what I can complete in one session. I'll prioritize by value, working through files in clusters, applying all fixes for each file, adding tests, then moving on. I'll batch the benchmark runs to stay efficient. But I should be transparent in my report about what gets fully implemented versus what gets deferred to later work.

My tier-one focus is the fail-closed correctness bugs that matter most: verdict.js disproof matching, gate depth and quote handling in llm/gate.js, entailment abstention and deadline clamping, router family rejection, browser observation on fetch failures, crawler script/style stripping for evidence integrity, and quote-match candidate validation.

I'm also tracking envelope closure in register-row, rejected specs and network event discrimination in propose.js, HTTP fetch failures in documents.js, end-date handling in ico.js, SRA row validation, entity form contradictions in identity.js, IPv6 address safety in safe-fetch.js, and pipeline stage failures triggering non-zero exits.

For tier 2, I need to harden the domain gates themselves—fixing budget-cap name binding, host parsing with computed members, deadline matching with Promise.race, and module-state tracking with Set/Map and scope narrowing. Each change risks breaking the gates' self-tests, so I'll need calibration fixtures for each.

Tier 3 involves pulling sector vocabulary imports into the coverage contract and register files, which is straightforward but touches many places.

Tier 4 focuses on evidence integrity across crawl, discover, documents, observe, and other modules—adding footer handling, budget caps, SSRF limits, deadline enforcement, and cookie logic.

Tier 5 wraps the e2e harness with fail-closed loaders in breach-stages, pipeline verification, and redteam error handling.

Tier 6 is dormant, covering docs and test syncs, while several fixes are deferred pending complexity refactors, legal verification, and calibration risk assessment.

The implementation surface is genuinely too large to complete responsibly in one turn with full test coverage and verification, so I'm focusing on the highest-value correctness bugs across Tiers 1-5 with tests, deferring the rest with reasoned explanations while keeping the fleet green. I should also reconsider whether parallel subagents could help with the volume here. I'm going to be pragmatic here and handle this myself—working through the tiers serially, verifying each change against the actual codebase, adding tests for the fail-closed logic, and keeping things stable. I'll push through as many tiers as the budget allows and draft deferred responses for the rest. This is the safest approach for a system that needs to be legally defensible. Now let me pull the GitHub Actions workflow data to see what I'm working with.

I need to gather some reconnaissance in parallel: checking the GHAS alerts from the affected sites, looking for any CodeRabbit configuration or agent path rules that might constrain how I approach this, and then reading through the key files I'll be fixing first—verdict.js, the LLM gate logic, entailment scoring, and the router.

=== path instructions in config ===
# yaml-language-server: $schema=https://coderabbit.ai/integrations/schema.v2.json
#
# CodeRabbit reviews EVERY pull request on this repo, automatically, 24/7, at the highest strictness.
# This file teaches it what "wrong" means HERE. This engine generates legal documents sent to real
# firms; a wrong fact is a false accusation with our name on it. The bug classes below all shipped
# to clients from the predecessor repo. CONSTITUTION.md and caution.md are the law of this repo:
# a diff that violates either is a defect regardless of whether the code "works".
language: en-GB
early_access: true

reviews:
  profile: assertive
  request_changes_workflow: true   # findings BLOCK the merge until resolved — the strict rule
  high_level_summary: true
  review_status: true
  poem: false
  collapse_walkthrough: false
  auto_review:
    enabled: true
    drafts: true
    base_branches: ["main"]

  path_instructions:
    - path: "**/*.js"
      instructions: |
        Read CONSTITUTION.md and caution.md first; every rule there is reviewable here. The stakes:
        this code produces legal claims quoted to law firms. Review for these classes FIRST:

        1. TWO DOORS. One client-visible fact (identity, jurisdiction, sector, regulator, fine,
           law title, host, element-checklist verdict) may have exactly ONE producer, declared in
           tools/one-door/facts.json. Any second derivation of such a fact, however small, is a
           defect. The stale door is the one the client sees.
        2. CATALOGUE-ONLY LAW FACTS. A fine amount, regulator name, law name, or statutory citation
           as a literal in source code is a defect even when currently correct. It must come from
           the catalogue artifact, which carries provenance and versioning.
        3. NO ARTIFACT, NO BREACH. Any code path that can emit a finding must attach a machine-
           verifiable evidence artifact (verbatim quote string-matched to the corpus, captured
           network event, register row, failing DOM node). Flag any finding constructed without one.
        4. FAIL CLOSED. Every gate must fail closed. Every catch must rethrow, record to the stage
           manifest/_warn, or carry a written `// FAIL-OPEN: <why the default IS the answer>`
           justification. A swallowed error ships a clean bill of health for a check that never ran.
        5. HOSTS ARE PARSED, NEVER SUBSTRINGS. `url.includes(domain)` matches
           'evil.com/?ref=reed.co.uk'. Only parsed-host comparison via the single url-safe module.
        6. ANCHOR EVERY REGEX ALTERNATION. `/^UAE|DIFC|ADGM/` anchors only the first branch.
           Over-escaped patterns (`dpo[@\\s]`) compile, match nothing forever, and report
           compliance. Any new/changed catalogue regex needs a positive-match fixture.
        7. STRING-LITERAL REQUIRES ONLY. Dynamic requires are invisible to the reachability gate —
           that is how correct legal logic sat dead in production for months.
        8. ABSENCE vs OBSERVATION. "It is missing" needs proof we looked (checked_urls or a proving
           page). "It happened" (tracker fired pre-consent) is proved by the captured observation.
           Conflating them either fabricates accusations or structurally blocks real breaches.
        9. MODULE-SCOPE STATE. No mutable module-level state that survives across mints (the _WARN
           singleton corrupted warning counts for every audit after the first). State is scoped per
           invocation.
        10. BUDGETS ARE CAPS, NEVER FLOORS. Any Math.max on a timeout, any retry loop without a
           hard deadline, any external call (LLM, browser, register API) without a bounded
           Promise.race is a defect.
        11. UNIFORM TAGS. Sector, sub-sector, jurisdiction, and nexus identifiers come ONLY from
           facts/vocabulary.js. A string literal that spells one of these vocabularies inline
           ("law-firms", "UK", "established_in") outside vocabulary.js or a fixture is a defect.
        12. LLM SELECTS, NEVER AUTHORS. Any LLM call whose output can CREATE a client-visible fact
           (rather than select from retrieved candidates, veto, or abstain) is a defect. Outputs
           must be schema-enforced and verbatim-quote-anchored; quotes re-verified by string match.

    - path: "eval/**"
      instructions: |
        These gates guard a legal document. A gate that encodes one spelling of a defect produces a
        CONFIDENT ZERO, which is worse than no gate because it stops anyone looking.
        - Prefer AST inspection over regex for code-structure checks.
        - A test that only reads source text cannot see a ReferenceError; ask whether the test
          EXECUTES the code path (two mint-killing bugs shipped past 77 green source-text evals).
        - Every new gate MUST land with a known-bad fixture in eval/calibration-known-bad/ and be
          wired into its run.js. A zero you did not earn is a lie.
        - Golden payloads change only via an explicit --accept in the same PR, with the diff stated
          in the PR description.

    - path: "catalogue/**"
      instructions: |
        The law registry. Every row/record must carry: statutory citation (act + section + official
        URL), penalty band (typical low/high + statutory max + currency), regulator, jurisdiction +
        sub-jurisdiction tags, sector/sub-sector tags from facts/vocabulary.js, applies_when AND
        excluded_when (thresholds like turnover exemptions are load-bearing: Modern Slavery was
        wrongly applied to SMEs in 5 of 9 forensic audits), required_nexus, and provenance
        (source, last_synced). A law without provenance must not merge. Polarity check: a
        prohibition rule breaches when the prohibited content IS present; five predecessor rules
        breached firms for NOT advertising what the law prohibits. Check the regex direction
        against the obligation text quoted in the record.

    - path: ".github/workflows/**"
      instructions: |
        Operational knobs are re-asserted in the workflow AFTER any .env/secret load; a secret
        silently overriding MINT_CONCURRENCY/engine version cost days. The engine version is a
        property of the code, never the environment. No workflow may reference a secret that makes
        a job fail red when absent; guard with if-expressions and log a loud skip.

    - path: "payload/**"
      instructions: |
        The payload schema IS the render contract (@tamazia/audit-contract). Any field
        added/removed/renamed here changes what a law firm sees: the same PR must update the
        schema version, the validator, the transform tests, and the golden payloads. The renderer
        re-derives NOTHING; if a reviewer can imagine the website computing this field itself,
        flag it.

  tools:
    semgrep: { enabled: true }
    eslint: { enabled: true }
    actionlint: { enabled: true }
    gitleaks: { enabled: true }
    yamllint: { enabled: true }
    markdownlint: { enabled: false }

chat:
  auto_reply: true

knowledge_base:
  learnings:
    scope: local
  issues:
    scope: local

content.thinking:
Looking at the CodeRabbit findings for the disproof matching logic in verdict.js, the issue is that checking only the first 40 characters of the disproof string against the evidence allows an attacker to craft a valid 40-character prefix followed by arbitrary fabricated text, which would still pass validation.

The fix is straightforward: require the entire normalized disproof to match verbatim in the evidence rather than just the prefix. This aligns with Gate 2 of the constitution, which mandates exact substring matching for quoted spans. While the original 40-character anchor was likely designed to tolerate model-generated trailing context or ellipsis, it creates a genuine vulnerability that violates the verbatim-quote requirement. Changing the check to match the full disproof string closes this loophole—legitimate verbatim spans still pass, but any disproof with fabricated content gets rejected.

Now I need to verify whether existing tests depend on the 40-character prefix behavior, since changing this could break tests that expect partial matches to pass. I should also decide whether to keep the `DISPROOF_ANCHOR_CHARS` constant exported or remove it entirely if it's no longer used after the fix.

Looking at entailment.js, I found another issue: the `numOr` function doesn't clamp the deadline to the maximum cap. When a deadline value is passed in, it bypasses the 9-second limit entirely. The fix would enforce that any deadline respects the cap using `Math.min`, but I need to clarify whether the default deadline is truly the maximum allowed or just a default starting point. The Math.min approach is fine since the budget-caps gate only flags Math.max floors, not caps. Now looking at the checkOne function on line 120—it currently guards against missing sourceId or premise, but doesn't check if the hypothesis is empty, so it could still call the model and return ok:true on an entailment response even when there's nothing to evaluate.

I need to add `!hypothesis` to that guard condition to align with the fail-closed principle—you can't entail something that doesn't exist. That's a clear fix.

The verdictFromResponse complexity issue on line 103 is marked as FIX-B since the health-gate doesn't actually flag it, only CodeScene does, so I'll defer that one. Now I'm going to review the test file and other related code before starting the edits.
